name: Blondie
description: Blondie emerged from the mid-1970s CBGB scene as pioneers of the new wave genre. Their early sound was an eclectic mixture incorporating elements of punk, disco, pop, and reggae. They initially gained underground status in the US while finding commercial success in the UK and Australia with their first two albums. By the late 1970s, they broke into the mainstream with the global success of the album Parallel Lines and its hit singles.
trivia: The band was originally billed as Angel and the Snake for two shows in August 1974 before becoming Blondie.
The name Blondie was derived from truck drivers who catcalled 'Hey, Blondie' to singer Debbie Harry.
Blondie opened for David Bowie and Iggy Pop on Iggy's 1977 US tour supporting The Idiot.

name: The Boomtown Rats
description: The Boomtown Rats formed in Dublin and became the first Irish rock band to achieve significant mainstream success in the UK. Their early sound was a mix of punk, new wave, and pub rock, driven by the charismatic stage presence of frontman Bob Geldof. They released a string of hit singles in the late 1970s that bridged the gap between punk attitude and pop accessibility. Their 1978 hit 'Rat Trap' was notably the first new wave song to reach number one on the UK singles chart.
trivia: The band name was taken from a boyhood gang mentioned in Woody Guthrie's autobiography Bound for Glory.
The band was originally called The Nightlife Thugs before Bob Geldof changed the name during their first gig on Halloween night.
Frontman Bob Geldof initially managed the band before taking over lead vocal duties.

name: Buzzcocks
description: English punk rock band formed by Pete Shelley and Howard Devoto who fused pop melodies with rapid-fire punk energy. They were pioneers in establishing independent record labels with their 'Spiral Scratch' EP in 1977. Their 1970s output was characterized by crisp melodies, driving guitars, and biting lyrics about adolescence and love. They achieved significant commercial success with singles that inspired the pop-punk and power pop movements.

key_albums_70s.title: Another Music in a Different Kitchen

key_songs_70s: Ever Fallen in Love (With Someone You Shouldn't've)
trivia: The band chose their name after reading a 'Time Out' headline 'It's the Buzz, Cock!'.
They were one of the first punk groups to establish an independent record label, New Hormones.
They made their debut opening for the Sex Pistols' second Manchester concert in 1976.

name: Dead Boys
description: Regarded as one of the rowdiest and most violent groups of the first wave of American punk. They moved to New York City from Cleveland at the encouragement of Joey Ramone and became fixtures at CBGB. Their 1970s sound was defined by outrageous live performances and high-energy anthems like 'Sonic Reducer'. The original lineup released two studio albums before disbanding in 1979.

key_albums_70s.title: We Have Come for Your Children

key_songs_70s: Caught with the Meat in Your Mouth
trivia: The band name was taken from a line in the Rocket from the Tombs song 'Down in Flames'.
Johnny Blitz was once stabbed five times in the chest during an altercation in Manhattan.
Their classic track 'Sonic Reducer' is often regarded as one of the great anthems of the punk genre.

name: Devo
description: Devo was formed in Akron, Ohio, centered around the tongue-in-cheek social theory of 'de-evolution'. Their early work featured experimental and dissonant art punk that merged rock music with electronic elements. They gained national exposure in 1978 following a high-profile appearance on Saturday Night Live. The band's visual presentation, including their signature costumes, was as integral to their identity as their satirical social commentary.

key_albums_70s.title: Q: Are We Not Men? A: We Are Devo!

trivia: The concept of 'de-evolution' was developed as a joke by Kent State University art students before the 1970 massacre made the idea more serious.
Their short film 'The Truth About De-Evolution' won a prize at the Ann Arbor Film Festival in 1976 and attracted the attention of David Bowie.
Devo's debut album was produced by Brian Eno after recommendations from David Bowie and Iggy Pop.

name: Elvis Costello
description: Elvis Costello emerged during the rise of punk rock in England, initially performing as a pub rock musician. His debut album My Aim Is True (1977) helped establish him as a literate and versatile songwriter. Along with his backing band the Attractions, he defined the British new wave sound with sharp wordplay and raw energy. By the late 1970s, he achieved peak pop stardom in the UK with a string of Top 30 hit singles.
trivia: Costello was working as a computer operator at a cosmetics factory while recording his debut album in 1977.
His stage name Elvis was chosen by his managers to reference Elvis Presley and attract attention.
In 1977, he was banned from American television for years after abruptly switching songs during a live performance on Saturday Night Live.

name: Gang of Four
description: Gang of Four formed in Leeds as a leading force in the late 1970s post-punk movement. Their sound featured a stripped-down mix of punk rock, funk, and dub, characterized by Andy Gill's angular guitar work. The band's lyrics were known for their analytical focus on social and political ills. Their 1979 debut album Entertainment! is widely considered one of the most influential records of the era.
trivia: The band was named after the 'Gang of Four' intra-party coup in China after a member saw a newspaper headline.
In 1979, the band walked off Top of the Pops after being told to change the word 'rubbers' in their lyrics.
Their debut single 'Damaged Goods' was a Number 1 indie chart hit and a favorite of radio DJ John Peel.

name: Gary Numan
description: Gary Numan first came to prominence as the frontman of the band Tubeway Army before launching a solo career. He is regarded as a pioneer of electronic music, moving from punk roots to a sound dominated by synthesizers and heavy guitar effects. His 'android' persona and distinctive voice helped him achieve massive commercial success in the late 1970s. Both with Tubeway Army and as a solo artist, he topped the UK charts with landmark singles like 'Are "Friends" Electric?' and 'Cars'.
trivia: Numan started out in the punk rock vein in 1977 but adopted the style solely to obtain a recording contract.
He accidentally created his signature look with white face makeup and black eye shadow before an appearance on Top of the Pops to hide acne.
The transition to his electronic sound began when he found a Moog synthesizer left behind in a recording studio.

name: Generation X
description: A London punk band that combined raw energy with commercially melodic sounds inspired by 1960s British pop. They were the musical starting point for frontman Billy Idol and were one of the first punk units to appear on 'Top of the Pops'. Their lyrics focused on adolescent concerns in West London rather than radical politics or cultural nihilism. They released two influential albums in the late 1970s before breaking up.
trivia: The band was named after a book title belonging to Billy Idol's mother.
They were the first band to play at the iconic London venue The Roxy.
Frontman Billy Idol resumed a performance in Derby after being punched in the face by a Hells Angel.

name: Joe Jackson
description: Joe Jackson is an English musician who achieved early success with the Joe Jackson Band in the late 1970s. His music during this period was a sophisticated mix of rock, melodic jazz, and new wave, often compared to contemporaries like Elvis Costello. His debut album Look Sharp! was a critical success, featuring sharp songwriting and energetic performances. He became a prominent figure in the new wave scene before transitioning into more jazz-inflected pop in the early 1980s.
key_songs_70s: Is She Really Going Out with Him?
trivia: He received the nickname 'Joe' because people thought he looked like the puppet character Joe 90, a genius child spy.
Jackson won a scholarship at age 18 to study musical composition at London's Royal Academy of Music.
His debut album Look Sharp! was ranked by Rolling Stone as one of the 100 best debut albums of all time.
youtube_search: Joe Jackson Is She Really Going Out with Him?

name: Nick Lowe
description: Nick Lowe was a pivotal figure in the 1970s UK music scene as a performer, songwriter, and producer. He was a leading member of the pub rock band Brinsley Schwarz before launching a solo career and co-founding Rockpile. As the in-house producer for Stiff Records, he helmed seminal punk and new wave recordings by artists like the Damned and Elvis Costello. His solo work in the late 1970s was characterized by a witty, energetic take on power pop and new wave.
key_songs_70s: I Love the Sound of Breaking Glass
trivia: Lowe's single 'So It Goes' was the first ever release on the legendary independent label Stiff Records.
His early 'rough and ready' production style earned him the nickname 'Basher'.
He wrote '(What's So Funny 'Bout) Peace, Love, and Understanding' while in the band Brinsley Schwarz before it became a hit for Elvis Costello.
youtube_search: Nick Lowe Cruel to Be Kind

name: Patti Smith
origin: New York City, New York, U.S.
description: Known as the 'punk poet laureate', she fused rock and roll with spoken-word poetry in the New York scene. Her 1975 debut album 'Horses' was a landmark release that made her an influential member of the New York punk rock movement. Her 1970s work with the Patti Smith Group combined raw garage rock energy with high-art intellectualism. She achieved mainstream success in 1978 with the hit single 'Because the Night'.
trivia: Smith worked in a factory before moving to New York, an experience that inspired her song 'Piss Factory'.
She co-wrote the hit song 'Because the Night' with Bruce Springsteen.
The cover of her debut album 'Horses' features a classic photograph by Robert Mapplethorpe.

name: Pere Ubu
description: Pere Ubu formed in Cleveland following the dissolution of the proto-punk band Rocket from the Tombs. Their work was inspired by avant-garde music, performance art, and the industrial landscape of the American Midwest. They rejected the punk rock label, instead describing their sound as 'avant-garage' and experimental rock. Their late 1970s output was critically acclaimed for its innovation and influence on the burgeoning underground and alternative rock scenes.
trivia: The band was named after the main character in the 1896 French avant-garde play Ubu Roi.
The members lived together in a five-story apartment building in Downtown Cleveland known as the Plaza.
Music journalist Jon Savage coined the term 'post-punk' in 1977 to describe Ohio bands like Pere Ubu.

name: Public Image Ltd
description: Public Image Ltd was formed by John Lydon after his departure from the Sex Pistols as an experimental 'anti-rock' project. Their debut studio album featured an abrasive, bass-heavy sound that blended punk with elements of dub and noise. The group became early pioneers of the post-punk movement, moving away from conventional rock structures. Their work in the late 1970s was characterized by Keith Levene's glassy guitar work and Jah Wobble's hypnotic, reggae-influenced bass lines.
trivia: Lydon named the band after the Muriel Spark novel The Public Image.
The album Metal Box was originally released as three untitled records inside a metal canister resembling a film can.
The debut single 'Public Image' was a direct attack on Sex Pistols manager Malcolm McLaren.

name: Ramones
origin: Forest Hills, Queens, New York, U.S.
description: Pioneers of the punk movement often recognized as one of the first true bands of the genre. Their 1970s sound was defined by short, fast, loud songs with minimal chords and catchy pop-influenced hooks. They set the blueprint for punk rock and pop-punk with a uniform look of leather jackets, torn jeans, and sneakers. Their first four albums are considered foundational works that changed the direction of popular music.
trivia: All members adopted the surname 'Ramone' despite not being biologically related.
The band's iconic logo was created by artist Arturo Vega and based on the Seal of the President of the United States.
Their debut album was recorded in just one week on a budget of $6,400.

name: Richard Hell and the Voidoids
origin: New York City, New York, U.S.
description: Led by former Television and Heartbreakers member Richard Hell, the band was a key part of the early New York punk scene. Hell's visual style of torn clothes and safety pins was highly influential on the punk look in both New York and London. Their 1970s sound was characterized by Robert Quine's innovative guitar work and Hell's poetic, nihilistic lyrics. Their debut album 'Blank Generation' is an underground classic of the era.
key_songs_70s: The Kid with the Replaceable Head
trivia: Richard Hell is credited with pioneering the punk look of torn clothing held together by safety pins.
The band was named after a novel Hell had been writing.
Drummer Marc Bell eventually joined the Ramones and changed his name to Marky Ramone.

name: Sex Pistols
description: The band that initiated the punk movement in the United Kingdom and became one of the most culturally influential acts in popular music. Their 1970s career was short but explosive, fueled by media scandals and provocative lyrics attacking the British monarchy and establishment. Their sole studio album, 'Never Mind the Bollocks', reached number one in the UK and is regarded as a seminal masterpiece. Their visual imagery and attitude redefined the punk subculture.

key_albums_70s.title: Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols

trivia: The band became a household name overnight after swearing during a live television interview with Bill Grundy.
Their single 'God Save the Queen' is considered the most censored record in British history.
The band was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame but refused to attend.
youtube_search: Sex Pistols Anarchy in the UK

name: Siouxsie and the Banshees
description: Emerging from the Bromley Contingent of Sex Pistols fans, they became one of the most innovative and uncompromising bands of the era. Their 1970s sound evolved from raw punk energy into a more complex, atmospheric style with rhythmic and sonic experimentation. They were a major influence on the development of gothic rock and the alternative music scene. Their debut album 'The Scream' received widespread critical acclaim upon its release in 1978.
trivia: The band's first live set at the 100 Club Punk Festival consisted of a 20-minute improvisation of 'The Lord's Prayer'.
Future Sex Pistol Sid Vicious played drums for the band's debut performance.
Their first single 'Hong Kong Garden' reached the UK top 10 shortly after its release.
youtube_search: Siouxsie and the Banshees Hong Kong Garden

name: Squeeze
description: Squeeze formed in London, becoming a prominent act in the British new wave scene of the late 1970s. The core songwriting duo of Chris Difford and Glenn Tilbrook were widely praised for their melodic pop sensibilities and detailed lyrical storytelling. Their early sound mixed post-punk energy with a classic pop craft that drew comparisons to Lennon and McCartney. They achieved significant chart success in the UK with several top-ten hits during the tail end of the decade.
trivia: The band name Squeeze was chosen as a facetious tribute to a 1973 album by the Velvet Underground.
Chris Difford formed the band after putting a card in a sweetshop window advertising for a guitarist for a band that didn't yet exist.
Their debut album and first EP were produced by John Cale, a founding member of the Velvet Underground.

name: Stiff Little Fingers
description: Formed during the height of the Troubles in Northern Ireland, their music was heavily informed by their experiences of conflict and repression. Their 1970s sound was raw and energetic, with lyrics addressing political and social issues facing youth in Belfast. Their debut album 'Inflammable Material' was the first independent LP to enter the UK top 20 charts. They were the first punk band from Belfast to release a record on their own label.
trivia: The band started out as a schoolboy rock cover band called Highway Star.
The single 'Suspect Device' was packaged to look like a cassette incendiary bomb.
They took their name from a song by the punk band The Vibrators.

name: Talking Heads
description: Talking Heads were a pivotal force in the New York punk and new wave scene, forming after the members met at art school. Their early music combined punk's energy with art rock and funk elements, complemented by David Byrne's distinctive vocal style. Collaborating with producer Brian Eno in the late 1970s, they explored increasingly diverse directions, including Afrobeat and world music influences. They were highly acclaimed for their intellectual yet groove-oriented sound and creative visual image.

key_albums_70s.title: More Songs About Buildings and Food

trivia: The band played their first ever show as Talking Heads opening for the Ramones at CBGB in 1975.
The name Talking Heads was inspired by a term used in TV studios to describe a head-and-shoulders shot of a person as 'all content, no action'.
Bassist Tina Weymouth learned to play her instrument by listening to Suzi Quatro albums at the encouragement of drummer Chris Frantz.

name: Television
origin: New York City, New York, U.S.
description: A foundational part of the New York punk scene at CBGB, Television's music was more technically proficient and improvisational than that of their contemporaries. Their 1970s sound featured clean, interlocking guitar parts and long, complex instrumental sections influenced by jazz and 1960s rock. Their 1977 debut album 'Marquee Moon' is widely acclaimed and considered one of the defining releases of the punk era. They disbanded in 1978 after releasing their second album, 'Adventure'.
trivia: The band name was a pun on 'tell a vision' devised by original member Richard Hell.
They were the first rock band to play the legendary New York club CBGB.
Guitarist Tom Verlaine once rejected demos produced by Brian Eno, calling the sound 'cold and brittle'.

name: The Cars
description: The Cars were a leading American rock band that emerged from the Boston new wave scene in the late 1970s. Their sound was a successful merger of guitar-oriented rock and synthesizer-driven pop, incorporating elements of punk minimalism and power pop. Their debut album was a massive commercial success, appearing on the charts for over two years and producing several hit singles. The band's image and sound helped define the transition from 70s rock to the new wave aesthetic of the early 1980s.
trivia: Drummer David Robinson suggested the name 'The Cars' and significantly influenced the band's visual image.
Their official debut show was played at Pease Air Force Base in New Hampshire on New Year's Eve, 1976.
The cover of their second album Candy-O was created by the famous Playboy artist Alberto Vargas.
youtube_search: The Cars Just What I Needed

name: The Clash
description: Often called 'The Only Band That Matters', they were a central force in British punk who infused their music with social consciousness and left-wing politics. Their 1970s sound expanded from raw punk to include elements of reggae, ska, and rockabilly. They achieved major critical and commercial success with their first three albums before the decade ended. They are recognized for their intense live performances and pioneering advocacy of radical politics in rock music.
trivia: Joe Strummer's stage name came from his rudimentary ukulele strumming skills as a busker.
Bassist Paul Simonon came up with the band name after seeing the word 'clash' repeatedly in newspapers.
The iconic cover of 'London Calling' features a photo of Paul Simonon smashing his bass.

name: The Damned
description: The first UK punk band to release a single and a studio album, and the first to tour the United States. Their 1970s sound was fast-driven and chaotic, helping to shape the emergence of hardcore punk. They were known for their aggressive attitude and Dave Vanian's vampire-themed stage persona. After briefly dissolving, they reformed to release the influential 'Machine Gun Etiquette' in 1979.
key_songs_70s: I Just Can't Be Happy Today
trivia: Their first single 'New Rose' was released five weeks before the Sex Pistols' debut.
They tried to recruit the reclusive Syd Barrett to produce their second album.
Lead singer Dave Vanian's vampire-themed appearance was a major influence on goth subculture.

name: The Jam
description: A leading force in the British mod revival that combined 1960s beat and soul influences with 1970s punk energy. Fronted by Paul Weller, their 1970s work was characterized by melodic pop structures and a distinctly English take on social commentary. They were known for their sharp mod image and achieved massive success on the UK charts with consecutive top 40 singles. They released four studio albums during the late 1970s.
key_songs_70s: Down in the Tube Station at Midnight
trivia: Paul Weller's father, John Weller, managed the band throughout their entire career.
The band was banned by Granada Television for ten years after taking a drum kit from a studio.
Weller wrote the hit 'That's Entertainment' in 15 minutes after returning home from a pub.

name: The Police
description: The Police formed in London as a power trio, emerging from the British punk scene before quickly expanding into new wave and reggae rock. Their early sound featured a nervous, reggae-injected energy that combined Sting's distinctive vocals with Stewart Copeland's inventive drumming. Their debut album reached the UK top ten and included songs that would become global standards. By the end of the 1970s, they had achieved massive international popularity and their first UK number-one singles.
key_songs_70s: The Bed's Too Big Without You
trivia: The band's trademark bleached-blond hair happened by accident after they were required to dye it for a chewing gum commercial.
Their debut album Outlandos d'Amour was recorded at night during off-peak hours to save money.
The single 'Roxanne' was promoted as 'Banned by the BBC' to gain attention, even though it was simply not play-listed.

name: The Pretenders
description: The Pretenders formed in London, centered around the songwriting and distinctive vocals of American-born Chrissie Hynde. Their sound integrated the raw energy of punk with melodic new wave structures, achieving rapid critical and commercial success. Their early singles at the end of the 1970s became instant hits in the UK and would soon break into the US charts. The band's debut album is widely regarded as one of the best records of the era, blending tough rock attitude with pop craft.
trivia: Founder Chrissie Hynde moved from Ohio to London in 1973 and worked at the NME and Malcolm McLaren's clothes store.
The band was named after the song 'The Great Pretender' by the Platters.
Hynde taught Sid Vicious how to play guitar before forming her own band.

name: The Slits
description: One of the first female-fronted punk bands, they eventually moved towards an experimental post-punk sound influenced by reggae and dub. Their 1970s live performances were raw and raucous, capturing the early spirit of the London punk scene. Their debut album 'Cut' is recognized as a defining work that combined punk attitude with world music rhythms. They are noted for their uncompromising attitude and influential role in the underground music scene.
key_songs_70s: I Heard It Through the Grapevine
trivia: The band formed after Ari Up met members at a Patti Smith concert at the Hammersmith Odeon.
The iconic cover of their debut album 'Cut' depicts the band members covered in mud.
Guitarist Viv Albertine was an early member who helped shape the band's distinctive sound.

name: The Stranglers
description: Originating in the pub rock scene, they became one of the most successful and longest-surviving bands to be associated with British punk. Their 1970s sound was driven by growling vocals and heavy, melodic basslines paired with prominent keyboard parts. They explored a variety of styles from aggressive punk to more sophisticated new wave and art rock. Despite friction with the music press, they built a massive fan following with hits like 'Peaches' and 'No More Heroes'.
trivia: Drummer Jet Black previously ran a fleet of ice cream vans which served as the band's early tour buses.
Bassist JJ Burnel once punched a music journalist for writing a negative review.
The band's 1982 smash hit 'Golden Brown' was inspired by heroin and a Mediterranean girl.

name: The Undertones
description: A Northern Irish band that mated infectious guitar hooks to adolescent themes like teenage angst and heartbreak. Their 1970s sound combined punk energy with melodic pop sensibilities, heavily influenced by the Ramones. They achieved breakthrough success with the song 'Teenage Kicks', which became a favorite of DJ John Peel. They remain the most successful band to emerge from Derry, capturing the essence of being a bored teenager in the late 1970s.
key_songs_70s: You've Got My Number (Why Don't You Use It?)
trivia: The song 'Teenage Kicks' was the all-time favorite song of BBC Radio 1 DJ John Peel.
The band formed in part because many entertainment venues in Derry were closed during the Troubles.
Lead singer Feargal Sharkey was a local Scout leader before the band achieved fame.

name: Ultravox
description: Ultravox originally formed as Tiger Lily, drawing early inspiration from glam rock before becoming a prominent fixture in the British new wave scene. Under original frontman John Foxx, the band explored experimental and art-school aesthetics, moving from punkier sounds to a pioneering synth-driven direction. Their late 1970s work was highly influential, being among the first British bands to prominently use drum machines and synthesizers. This period set the stage for their massive commercial success in the synth-pop era of the early 1980s.
key_songs_70s: I Want to Be a Machine
trivia: The exclamation mark in their early name 'Ultravox!' was a reference to the krautrock band Neu!
The band used the name 'The Damned' for a few weeks before discovering another group had already taken it.
The song 'Hiroshima Mon Amour' was one of the first tracks by a British band to feature a drum machine.

name: Wire
description: Pioneers of the post-punk and art punk genres known for their constant experimentation with song structures and minimalist approach. Their 1970s work began with the stark, short songs of 'Pink Flag' and evolved into a more complex sound using synthesizers and guitar effects. They are regarded as one of the most influential bands of the original punk wave, influencing hardcore punk and later alternative rock. Their output is characterized by unorthodox ideas and a refusal to follow standard rock conventions.
trivia: Their debut album 'Pink Flag' contains 21 songs, many of which are under two minutes long.
The band fell out with EMI after the label refused to promote their album '154' with theater events.
The song 'Connection' by Elastica was the subject of a plagiarism settlement with Wire.

name: X-Ray Spex
description: Led by the iconic Poly Styrene, they were one of the most inventive and genuinely exciting groups to emerge during the punk era. Their 1970s sound was unique for its use of the saxophone and Styrene's discordant, high-energy vocals. Her lyrics dealt with thoughtful themes of anti-consumerism, synthetic fashions, and individual expression. Their debut album 'Germfree Adolescents' is acclaimed as a classic of the British punk movement.
key_songs_70s: The Day the World Turned Day-Glo
trivia: Poly Styrene was inspired to form the band after watching the Sex Pistols perform in Hastings.
She once shaved her head at Johnny Rotten's flat prior to a major concert.
The song 'Oh Bondage Up Yours!' is regarded as a foundational anthem of the riot grrrl movement.
youtube_search: X-Ray Spex Oh Bondage Up Yours!

name: XTC
description: XTC formed in Swindon and gained early popularity during the rise of punk and new wave with their energetic live performances. They refused to play conventional punk, instead synthesizing influences from ska, 60s pop, and the avant-garde into an angular guitar-pop sound. Their late 1970s work was characterized by quirky wordplay, sharp arrangements, and the innovative use of keyboards and complex rhythms. The single 'Making Plans for Nigel' marked their commercial breakthrough and helped define the sound of the new decade.
trivia: Andy Partridge derived the name 'XTC' from an exclamation by comedian Jimmy Durante.
The band was banned from BBC radio for the song 'Statue of Liberty' due to the lyrics about sailing beneath a skirt.
The distinctive drum sound on 'Making Plans for Nigel' was an attempt to invert traditional drum patterns.
